file.content:
# examples/projects/medium/project.yaml — Phase 5 D-B2 medium sample (~$5 real Claude).
#
# This sample lets operators run TIDE against real Claude (Haiku 4.5, cost-disciplined
# per D-B1) with a hard $5 cap — no external public repo dependency (D-B3). The
# targetRepo is an in-cluster http:// git remote served by the tide-git-http-server
# Deployment (git http-backend CGI + nginx), backed by the demo-remote-pvc populated
# by the demo-remote-init Job. All git transport is pure-Go (go-git HTTP) — no git
# binary in core images required.
#
# APPLY ORDER (do NOT apply this file alone — see README.md):
#   1. namespace.yaml
#   2. demo-remote-pvc.yaml
#   3. per-namespace-resources.yaml      (tide-projects PVC + tide-subagent SA)
#   4. Mirror tide-signing-key Secret from tide-system (see per-namespace-resources.yaml)
#   5. demo-remote-init-job.yaml         (init Job populates the bare repo on demo-remote-pvc)
#      kubectl wait --for=condition=Complete job/demo-remote-init \
#        -n tide-sample-medium --timeout=2m
#   6. git-http-server-deployment.yaml   (after init Job completes — RWO PVC ordering)
#      kubectl wait --for=condition=Available deployment/git-http-server \
#        -n tide-sample-medium --timeout=2m
#   7. kubectl create secret generic tide-secrets \
#        --from-literal=ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY" \
#        --from-literal=GIT_PAT="" \   # empty — anonymous in-cluster http:// push
#        -n tide-sample-medium
#   8. project.yaml                      (THIS file)
#   9. kubectl wait --for=jsonpath='{.status.phase}'=Complete \
#        project/medium-project -n tide-sample-medium --timeout=30m
#
# Schema notes:
#   - ProjectSpec.OutcomePrompt is a first-class Spec field read by the planner subagent.
#   - targetRepo uses http:// in-cluster URL (file:// rejected by CEL validator in Phase 8).
#   - GIT_PAT can be empty — tide-push push mode GIT_PAT guard is scheme-conditional
#     (Phase 8 08-05 Task 3): empty PAT is accepted for anonymous http:// remotes.
---
apiVersion: tideproject.k8s/v1alpha1
kind: Project
metadata:
  name: medium-project
  namespace: tide-sample-medium
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: tide
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: tide-sample
    tideproject.k8s/sample: medium
spec:
  # In-cluster anonymous git remote served by git-http-server Deployment + ClusterIP Service.
  # Transport: go-git HTTP (pure-Go, no git binary in tide-push or claude-subagent images).
  # Service DNS: http://git-http-server.tide-sample-medium.svc.cluster.local/demo-remote.git
  # Short form (same namespace): http://git-http-server/demo-remote.git
  targetRepo: http://git-http-server.tide-sample-medium.svc.cluster.local/demo-remote.git

  # Medium-sample outcome prompt — small enough to fit comfortably under the
  # $5 cap (D-B1) yet substantive enough to exercise TIDE's clone → plan →
  # execute → push loop end-to-end. ONE Phase, ONE Plan, target 3-5 tasks
  # (tight scope is the cost-control mechanism — same shape as the large
  # sample's Variant B per D-B4). Field is `spec.outcomePrompt` (added
  # 2026-05-23 root-cause fix; previously carried as annotation in Wave 3
  # Plan 05-12 — that workaround is now reverted).
  outcomePrompt: |
    The targetRepo is a tiny Go scaffold from examples/tide-demo-fixture/
    with a `Greeting(name) string` function plus a passing test.

    Add a second exported function alongside `Greeting`: `FormattedNow() string`
    that returns the current time formatted as RFC3339. Update main.go to
    print both functions' output (one per line). Add a unit test for the
    new function that asserts the returned string parses back via
    time.Parse(time.RFC3339, ...).

    Concrete deliverables (tight scope — ONE Phase, ONE Plan, target 3-5
    tasks):

    1. main.go — add `FormattedNow()`; update main() to print both.
    2. main_test.go — add `TestFormattedNow` asserting round-trip parse.

    Constraints:
    - DO NOT add external dependencies beyond the standard library.
    - DO NOT modify go.mod / go.sum.
    - Follow the existing repo conventions (MIT license header on new
      Go files; deterministic, side-effect-free function shape).

    Pass criterion: `go test ./...` is green inside the cloned working tree.

  # Phase 2 D-C1..C4: credproxy reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from this Secret.
  # The Secret is created BEFORE this CRD is applied (README.md step 7).
  # Same Secret carries GIT_PAT="" (empty — anonymous in-cluster http:// push
  # is accepted by the scheme-conditional guard in cmd/tide-push push mode).
  providerSecretRef: tide-secrets

  # D-B1: $5 cap for the medium sample. If hit, dispatch halts with
  # Phase=BudgetExceeded (Phase 2 D-D2 + Phase 04.1 P4.1 rolling-window
  # infra). The tight outcome-prompt scope bounds expected spend well
  # under this cap — the cap is a safety net, not the operating budget.
  budget:
    absoluteCapCents: 500
    rollingWindowCapCents: 500
    # 24h rolling window per Phase 04.1 P4.1. metav1.Duration parses Go
    # duration strings; default would be 24h when unset; explicit per
    # Pitfall 7.
    rollingWindowDuration: 24h

  # Phase 3 D-C2: cost-disciplined model pick per D-B1 spectrum. Haiku 4.5
  # is materially cheaper than Sonnet/Opus and adequate for the
  # function-addition outcome prompt above. Acceptance test uses Opus
  # (large sample); medium uses Haiku.
  subagent:
    image: ghcr.io/jsquirrelz/tide-claude-subagent:1.0.0
    model: claude-haiku-4-5

  # Phase 3 D-B6 per-project git target. TIDE pushes per-Phase / per-Plan /
  # per-Task / final-Project commits to a per-run branch on this remote.
  # Branch shape: tide/run-medium-project-<unix-ts>. NEVER pushes to main.
  #
  # The same `tide-secrets` Secret carries GIT_PAT="" (empty, accepted for
  # anonymous http:// remotes by the scheme-conditional guard in tide-push).
  git:
    repoURL: http://git-http-server.tide-sample-medium.svc.cluster.local/demo-remote.git
    credsSecretRef: tide-secrets

  # All gates set to `auto` so the medium sample runs end-to-end without
  # human interruption. Operators inspecting the run watch
  # Project.Status.phase + the dashboard; gate-policy demos are a docs/
  # concern, not this sample's purpose.
  gates:
    milestone: auto
    phase: auto
    plan: auto
    task: auto
    pauseBetweenWaves: false

  # Phase 2 D-E1: file-touch admission in strict mode. The PLAN.md the
  # planner authors must declare its file touches precisely; admission
  # rejects on mismatch.
  planAdmission:
    fileTouchMode: strict

  # Phase 2 SUB-04 cap. 3 attempts per task is the v1 default.
  maxAttemptsPerTask: 3

content.content:
# examples/projects/dogfood/02-codex-runtime-project.yaml
#
# Run 2 of 3 in the TIDE-on-TIDE dogfood sequence.
# Apply ONLY after 01-analytics-project reaches `status.phase: Complete`.
# Do NOT apply 03-project-editor-project.yaml until this Project reaches
# `status.phase: Complete` — the Project CRD has no ordering mechanism;
# sequencing is operator discipline.
#
# Purpose: add a second Subagent implementation (Codex / OpenAI) to TIDE and
# demonstrate real heterogeneous dispatch — actually running mixed-provider
# waves, not just proving the interface compiles.
#
# After this run completes: the run's token spend, dispatch behavior, and
# failure characteristics are visible on the analytics dashboard built by run 1.
# Run 3 (Project editor) can then be authored and applied through the dashboard
# surface run 3 itself will build.
#
# Prerequisites (see README.md for full recipe):
#   - 01-analytics-project must have reached status.phase: Complete
#   - kind cluster + TIDE charts installed (see docs/INSTALL.md)
#   - tide-secrets Secret created in namespace tide-dogfood-codex
#     (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY + GIT_PAT — same as analytics namespace)
#   - openai-secrets Secret created in namespace tide-dogfood-codex
#     (OPENAI_API_KEY or CODEX_API_KEY — see README.md)
#   - per-namespace-resources applied per README.md
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: tide-dogfood-codex
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: tide
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: tide-sample
    tideproject.k8s/sample: dogfood-codex
---
apiVersion: tideproject.k8s/v1alpha1
kind: Project
metadata:
  name: dogfood-codex-runtime
  namespace: tide-dogfood-codex
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: tide
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: tide-sample
    tideproject.k8s/sample: dogfood-codex
spec:
  # TIDE operates against its own repo — TIDE-on-TIDE dogfood run.
  targetRepo: https://github.com/jsquirrelz/tide.git

  outcomePrompt: |
    ## Outcome

    Add a second Subagent implementation for the Codex runtime (OpenAI) and
    demonstrate real heterogeneous dispatch — mixed-provider waves that actually
    run, not just interface scaffolding. Concrete outcome: a TIDE operator can
    configure a Project where the planner pool runs Claude and the executor pool
    runs Codex, and TIDE dispatches a multi-phase run to completion with correct
    wave-boundary failure semantics across both providers.

    ## Architecture — locked decisions (do NOT reinvent these)

    **Implementation location: internal/subagent/codex/**
    Mirror the existing internal/subagent/anthropic/ layering exactly:
    - internal/subagent/codex/client.go — Client struct + constructor
    - internal/subagent/codex/run.go — Subagent.Run(ctx, EnvelopeIn) (EnvelopeOut, error)
    - internal/subagent/codex/Dockerfile — multi-stage build mirroring anthropic/Dockerfile
    - internal/subagent/codex/doc.go — package doc referencing D-C1 layering pattern

    All OpenAI/Codex-specific code stays behind the pkg/dispatch.Subagent
    interface. No provider hard-coding in the controller. No Codex imports
    in internal/controller/ or cmd/manager/.

    **Per-level runtime selection (not per-task, not per-project).**
    Planner pool and executor pool each pin a runtime. This is the correct
    granularity: it matches the spec's separate-pools argument, achieves real
    heterogeneity within a single run, and avoids the API/scheduler complexity
    of per-task selection. Rejected alternatives (do not reopen):
    - Per-task selection: large API surface, non-trivial scheduler changes.
    - Per-project selection: no mixing within a run (defeats heterogeneity goal).

    The SubagentConfig field already has Levels.{Milestone,Phase,Plan,Task}
    per-level LevelConfig overrides (api/v1alpha1/project_types.go). Wire the
    Codex image at the levels the operator wants to run Codex.

    **Credential path.**
    OPENAI_API_KEY or CODEX_API_KEY via a K8s Secret — the established
    Secret-ref pattern from providerSecretRef. Never mount host config, never
    headless OAuth (broken: claude-code#29983, #7100 — same restriction applies
    to Codex). Chart values for the new Secret name are part of the deliverable.

    **Mixed-provider waves: identical wave-boundary failure semantics.**
    The spec's failure handling (spec section "Failure handling at wave
    boundaries") applies unchanged regardless of which provider runs a task:
    - Failed task: siblings in the same wave continue (they were declared
      independent).
    - Dependents in later waves: never dispatch.
    - Non-dependents in later waves: dispatch in strict profile.
    This contract must not change when tasks run on different providers.

    **Codex CLI facts — carry verbatim, do NOT re-research:**
    - codex exec is first-class headless: one-shot, no TTY, final message
      to stdout.
    - Flags for clean containers: --ephemeral, --skip-git-repo-check,
      --ignore-user-config.
    - --json emits a JSONL event stream.
    - --output-schema <file> constrains the final response to a JSON schema
      — use this for child-CRD emission (child-CRD format is JSON, mandated).
    - Sandbox defaults read-only; the runner needs --sandbox workspace-write
      (fine inside an already-isolated pod — mirrors the Claude runner posture).
    - API-key env auth (OPENAI_API_KEY) is the documented container path —
      no headless-OAuth problem.
    - MCP client support (STDIO + streamable HTTP) via config.toml or
      codex mcp add — available if needed for child-CRD emission.
    - Sources: developers.openai.com/codex/{noninteractive,cli/reference,
      auth,cli/features} (verified 2026-06).

    **Container image.**
    internal/subagent/codex/Dockerfile must build clean. The final image
    bundles the Codex CLI binary. Image reference convention mirrors
    ghcr.io/jsquirrelz/tide-claude-subagent:1.0.0 (replace "claude" with
    "codex" in the image name; exact tag/registry are authoring decisions).

    ## Constraints

    - All Codex/OpenAI-specific code stays in internal/subagent/codex/ and
      behind the pkg/dispatch.Subagent interface — no provider leakage into
      the controller or cmd/manager.
    - Wave-boundary failure semantics unchanged for mixed-provider waves.
    - Secret material never inlined in manifests or chart values — reference
      by Secret name only.
    - Follow all CLAUDE.md conventions: Apache 2.0 headers, logr/zap logging,
      controller-runtime patterns.
    - Do NOT break existing tests. Confirm make test is green before
      authoring any plan as delivered.
    - Do NOT add a real OpenAI API call in tests without an explicit test-env
      guard (OPENAI_API_KEY env set) — tests must pass offline.

  # Phase 2 D-C1..C4: credproxy reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from this Secret.
  # For the planner pool (Claude side of the heterogeneous run).
  # The Codex executor pool's OPENAI_API_KEY lives in a separate Secret —
  # see README.md for the openai-secrets creation step.
  providerSecretRef: tide-secrets

  # Starting cap for dogfood runs — tune before applying.
  # These are multi-phase real work runs; the cap is a safety net.
  budget:
    absoluteCapCents: 10000
    rollingWindowCapCents: 10000
    rollingWindowDuration: 24h

  # Default image + model for the planner pool (Claude).
  # Codex executor pool is wired via subagent.levels.task.image override
  # once the codex subagent image is built by this run.
  # Cheaper alternatives: model: claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-haiku-4-5
  subagent:
    image: ghcr.io/jsquirrelz/tide-claude-subagent:1.0.0
    model: claude-opus-4-7

  # Phase 3 D-B6: per-project git target + creds.
  git:
    repoURL: https://github.com/jsquirrelz/tide.git
    credsSecretRef: tide-secrets

  # Human oversight at milestone and phase level; plan and task auto.
  # Fully-autonomous: change milestone and phase to auto.
  gates:
    milestone: approve
    phase: approve
    plan: auto
    task: auto
    pauseBetweenWaves: false

  planAdmission:
    fileTouchMode: strict

  maxAttemptsPerTask: 3

tool_use_result.stdout:
# examples/projects/dogfood/02-codex-runtime-project.yaml
#
# Run 2 of 3 in the TIDE-on-TIDE dogfood sequence.
# Apply ONLY after 01-analytics-project reaches `status.phase: Complete`.
# Do NOT apply 03-project-editor-project.yaml until this Project reaches
# `status.phase: Complete` — the Project CRD has no ordering mechanism;
# sequencing is operator discipline.
#
# Purpose: add a second Subagent implementation (Codex / OpenAI) to TIDE and
# demonstrate real heterogeneous dispatch — actually running mixed-provider
# waves, not just proving the interface compiles.
#
# After this run completes: the run's token spend, dispatch behavior, and
# failure characteristics are visible on the analytics dashboard built by run 1.
# Run 3 (Project editor) can then be authored and applied through the dashboard
# surface run 3 itself will build.
#
# Prerequisites (see README.md for full recipe):
#   - 01-analytics-project must have reached status.phase: Complete
#   - kind cluster + TIDE charts installed (see docs/INSTALL.md)
#   - tide-secrets Secret created in namespace tide-dogfood-codex
#     (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY + GIT_PAT — same as analytics namespace)
#   - openai-secrets Secret created in namespace tide-dogfood-codex
#     (OPENAI_API_KEY or CODEX_API_KEY — see README.md)
#   - per-namespace-resources applied per README.md
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: tide-dogfood-codex
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: tide
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: tide-sample
    tideproject.k8s/sample: dogfood-codex
---
apiVersion: tideproject.k8s/v1alpha1
kind: Project
metadata:
  name: dogfood-codex-runtime
  namespace: tide-dogfood-codex
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: tide
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: tide-sample
    tideproject.k8s/sample: dogfood-codex
spec:
  # TIDE operates against its own repo — TIDE-on-TIDE dogfood run.
  targetRepo: https://github.com/jsquirrelz/tide.git

  outcomePrompt: |
    ## Outcome

    Add a second Subagent implementation for the Codex runtime (OpenAI) and
    demonstrate real heterogeneous dispatch — mixed-provider waves that actually
    run, not just interface scaffolding. Concrete outcome: a TIDE operator can
    configure a Project where the planner pool runs Claude and the executor pool
    runs Codex, and TIDE dispatches a multi-phase run to completion with correct
    wave-boundary failure semantics across both providers.

    ## Architecture — locked decisions (do NOT reinvent these)

    **Implementation location: internal/subagent/codex/**
    Mirror the existing internal/subagent/anthropic/ layering exactly:
    - internal/subagent/codex/client.go — Client struct + constructor
    - internal/subagent/codex/run.go — Subagent.Run(ctx, EnvelopeIn) (EnvelopeOut, error)
    - internal/subagent/codex/Dockerfile — multi-stage build mirroring anthropic/Dockerfile
    - internal/subagent/codex/doc.go — package doc referencing D-C1 layering pattern

    All OpenAI/Codex-specific code stays behind the pkg/dispatch.Subagent
    interface. No provider hard-coding in the controller. No Codex imports
    in internal/controller/ or cmd/manager/.

    **Per-level runtime selection (not per-task, not per-project).**
    Planner pool and executor pool each pin a runtime. This is the correct
    granularity: it matches the spec's separate-pools argument, achieves real
    heterogeneity within a single run, and avoids the API/scheduler complexity
    of per-task selection. Rejected alternatives (do not reopen):
    - Per-task selection: large API surface, non-trivial scheduler changes.
    - Per-project selection: no mixing within a run (defeats heterogeneity goal).

    The SubagentConfig field already has Levels.{Milestone,Phase,Plan,Task}
    per-level LevelConfig overrides (api/v1alpha1/project_types.go). Wire the
    Codex image at the levels the operator wants to run Codex.

    **Credential path.**
    OPENAI_API_KEY or CODEX_API_KEY via a K8s Secret — the established
    Secret-ref pattern from providerSecretRef. Never mount host config, never
    headless OAuth (broken: claude-code#29983, #7100 — same restriction applies
    to Codex). Chart values for the new Secret name are part of the deliverable.

    **Mixed-provider waves: identical wave-boundary failure semantics.**
    The spec's failure handling (spec section "Failure handling at wave
    boundaries") applies unchanged regardless of which provider runs a task:
    - Failed task: siblings in the same wave continue (they were declared
      independent).
    - Dependents in later waves: never dispatch.
    - Non-dependents in later waves: dispatch in strict profile.
    This contract must not change when tasks run on different providers.

    **Codex CLI facts — carry verbatim, do NOT re-research:**
    - codex exec is first-class headless: one-shot, no TTY, final message
      to stdout.
    - Flags for clean containers: --ephemeral, --skip-git-repo-check,
      --ignore-user-config.
    - --json emits a JSONL event stream.
    - --output-schema <file> constrains the final response to a JSON schema
      — use this for child-CRD emission (child-CRD format is JSON, mandated).
    - Sandbox defaults read-only; the runner needs --sandbox workspace-write
      (fine inside an already-isolated pod — mirrors the Claude runner posture).
    - API-key env auth (OPENAI_API_KEY) is the documented container path —
      no headless-OAuth problem.
    - MCP client support (STDIO + streamable HTTP) via config.toml or
      codex mcp add — available if needed for child-CRD emission.
    - Sources: developers.openai.com/codex/{noninteractive,cli/reference,
      auth,cli/features} (verified 2026-06).

    **Container image.**
    internal/subagent/codex/Dockerfile must build clean. The final image
    bundles the Codex CLI binary. Image reference convention mirrors
    ghcr.io/jsquirrelz/tide-claude-subagent:1.0.0 (replace "claude" with
    "codex" in the image name; exact tag/registry are authoring decisions).

    ## Constraints

    - All Codex/OpenAI-specific code stays in internal/subagent/codex/ and
      behind the pkg/dispatch.Subagent interface — no provider leakage into
      the controller or cmd/manager.
    - Wave-boundary failure semantics unchanged for mixed-provider waves.
    - Secret material never inlined in manifests or chart values — reference
      by Secret name only.
    - Follow all CLAUDE.md conventions: Apache 2.0 headers, logr/zap logging,
      controller-runtime patterns.
    - Do NOT break existing tests. Confirm make test is green before
      authoring any plan as delivered.
    - Do NOT add a real OpenAI API call in tests without an explicit test-env
      guard (OPENAI_API_KEY env set) — tests must pass offline.

  # Phase 2 D-C1..C4: credproxy reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from this Secret.
  # For the planner pool (Claude side of the heterogeneous run).
  # The Codex executor pool's OPENAI_API_KEY lives in a separate Secret —
  # see README.md for the openai-secrets creation step.
  providerSecretRef: tide-secrets

  # Starting cap for dogfood runs — tune before applying.
  # These are multi-phase real work runs; the cap is a safety net.
  budget:
    absoluteCapCents: 10000
    rollingWindowCapCents: 10000
    rollingWindowDuration: 24h

  # Default image + model for the planner pool (Claude).
  # Codex executor pool is wired via subagent.levels.task.image override
  # once the codex subagent image is built by this run.
  # Cheaper alternatives: model: claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-haiku-4-5
  subagent:
    image: ghcr.io/jsquirrelz/tide-claude-subagent:1.0.0
    model: claude-opus-4-7

  # Phase 3 D-B6: per-project git target + creds.
  git:
    repoURL: https://github.com/jsquirrelz/tide.git
    credsSecretRef: tide-secrets

  # Human oversight at milestone and phase level; plan and task auto.
  # Fully-autonomous: change milestone and phase to auto.
  gates:
    milestone: approve
    phase: approve
    plan: auto
    task: auto
    pauseBetweenWaves: false

  planAdmission:
    fileTouchMode: strict

  maxAttemptsPerTask: 3

```go
    return pkgdispatch.ProviderSpec{

```

The `LevelConfig` struct already has an `Image` field but no `Vendor` field. The prompt notes that `resolveImage` already honors per-level images, but `ResolveProvider` still hardcodes the vendor to Anthropic, which causes non-Anthropic images to be rejected at runtime. I need to either derive the vendor from the image reference or add a `Vendor` field to `LevelConfig` to fix this.

Looking at the architecture more carefully, the cleanest approach is to add a `Vendor` field to `LevelConfig` and have `ResolveProvider` use it, rather than trying to infer the vendor from the image name. This aligns with the requirement to "wire the Codex image at the levels the operator wants Codex."

I need to build the Codex subagent package with a client, pricing tables for different models, the core Run implementation, and a parser for the JSONL event stream that Codex outputs. For testing, I need unit tests for the codex subagent package and tests that verify the per-level vendor resolution works correctly. 12	WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
13	See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
14	limitations under the License.
15	*/
16	
17	// Env-variable helpers for Helm-driven configuration (Phase 3 plan 03-09).
18	//
19	// These helpers exist as testable units (cmd/manager/env_test.go) because
20	// the main() function itself is not unit-testable (it constructs the
21	// controller-runtime Manager and blocks on mgr.Start). Helpers cover the
22	// per-level model defaults (D-C4) and leader-election tuning (D-D1).
23	package main
24	
25	import (
26		"os"
27		"strconv"
28		"time"
29	
30		"github.com/jsquirrelz/tide/internal/controller"
31	)
32	
33	// envOrDefault returns the value of the env var named key, or fallback when
34	// the env var is unset or empty. Empty-string is treated as unset so a Helm
35	// value left at its zero default (e.g. `value: ""`) cleanly falls through to
36	// the binary's compile-time default rather than overriding it with "".
37	func envOrDefault(key, fallback string) string {
38		if v := os.Getenv(key); v != "" {
39			return v
40		}
41		return fallback
42	}
43	
44	// atoiOrDefault returns the integer value of the env var named key, or
45	// fallback when the env var is unset, empty, or not an integer.
46	// Non-integer values are tolerated (return fallback) so a stray non-numeric
47	// value in the Helm chart cannot crash-loop the controller.
48	func atoiOrDefault(key string, fallback int) int {
49		v := os.Getenv(key)
50		if v == "" {
51			return fallback
52		}
53		n, err := strconv.Atoi(v)
54		if err != nil {
55			return fallback
56		}
57		return n
58	}
59	
60	// resolvePerLevelModels returns the four-key map from level name
61	// ("milestone"|"phase"|"plan"|"task") to the model identifier used as the
62	// last-fallback in dispatch_helpers.ResolveProvider (D-C2).
63	//
64	// Defaults match D-C4 — phase brief lock-in:
65	//
66	//	milestone → claude-opus-4-7    (heavy planning, lowest fan-out)
67	//	phase     → claude-sonnet-4-6
68	//	plan      → claude-sonnet-4-6
69	//	task      → claude-haiku-4-5   (highest fan-out, cost-bounded)
70	//
71	// Override per-level via env vars (set by the manager Deployment from Helm
72	// values.yaml subagent.levels.{level}.model):
73	//
74	//	TIDE_DEFAULT_MODEL_MILESTONE
75	//	TIDE_DEFAULT_MODEL_PHASE
76	//	TIDE_DEFAULT_MODEL_PLAN
77	//	TIDE_DEFAULT_MODEL_TASK
78	func resolvePerLevelModels() map[string]string {
79		return map[string]string{
80			"milestone": envOrDefault("TIDE_DEFAULT_MODEL_MILESTONE", "claude-opus-4-7"),
81			"phase":     envOrDefault("TIDE_DEFAULT_MODEL_PHASE", "claude-sonnet-4-6"),
82			"plan":      envOrDefault("TIDE_DEFAULT_MODEL_PLAN", "claude-sonnet-4-6"),
83			"task":      envOrDefault("TIDE_DEFAULT_MODEL_TASK", "claude-haiku-4-5"),
84		}
85	}
86	
87	// tideHelmProviderDefaults builds the controller.ProviderDefaults struct
88	// (D-C2 last-fallback in ResolveProvider) from the Helm-chart subagent image
89	// + per-level model map. claudeImage is the default subagent image
90	// (CLAUDE_SUBAGENT_IMAGE env var); per-level models come from
91	// resolvePerLevelModels (TIDE_DEFAULT_MODEL_* env vars).
92	//
93	// Called once at Manager startup; the result is stamped onto each up-stack
94	// reconciler's HelmProviderDefaults field (MilestoneReconciler /
95	// PhaseReconciler / PlanReconciler).
96	func tideHelmProviderDefaults(claudeImage string) controller.ProviderDefaults {
97		return controller.ProviderDefaults{
98			Image:  claudeImage,
99			Models: resolvePerLevelModels(),
100		}
101	}
102	
103	// resolveLeaderElectionTiming returns the (lease, renew, retry) durations
104	// for controller-runtime's LeaderElection block (D-D1 / Phase 3 chaos-resume).
105	//
106	// Defaults pin to common controller-runtime production values
107	// (lease=15s, renew=10s, retry=2s) which give a single-pod restart ≤25s
108	// failover ceiling, with the invariant lease > renew > retry preserved.
109	// Override via env vars (set by manager Deployment from Helm values.yaml
110	// leaderElection.*):
111	//
112	//	TIDE_LEADER_LEASE_SECONDS  (default 15)
113	//	TIDE_LEADER_RENEW_SECONDS  (default 10)
114	//	TIDE_LEADER_RETRY_SECONDS  (default 2)
115	func resolveLeaderElectionTiming() (lease, renew, retry time.Duration) {
116		lease = time.Duration(atoiOrDefault("TIDE_LEADER_LEASE_SECONDS", 15)) * time.Second
117		renew = time.Duration(atoiOrDefault("TIDE_LEADER_RENEW_SECONDS", 10)) * time.Second
118		retry = time.Duration(atoiOrDefault("TIDE_LEADER_RETRY_SECONDS", 2)) * time.Second
119		return lease, renew, retry
120	}
121

The key insight here is that while `resolveImage` already
respects per-level image overrides, `ResolveProvider` currently returns a single
vendor for all levels, so I need to align that behavior to allow
vendor selection at each level.
The clean
est approach is adding a `Vendor` field directly to `LevelConfig` rather than trying to derive it from the image name, which would be fragile.

field, fall back to the image name if needed, then helmDefaults vendor, and finally default
to "anthropic". I'm looking at the `ProviderDefaults` struct to understand
how to wire this through properly.

ifying the constraint: the planner pool (milestone/phase/plan levels) uses
Claude, while the executor pool (task level) uses Codex. So adding `Vendor` to `LevelConfig` is the right approach.
The `ProviderDefaults` struct should include a `Vendors` map similar to `Models`, but since the per
-level image already encodes the vendor choice (tide-codex-subagent for Codex, tide-claude-subagent for Anthropic), the operator can explicitly declare
the vendor via the `LevelConfig.Vendor` field when needed.
I
could also derive the vendor from the image name itself—images containing "codex" would map
to Codex, and those with "claude" would map to Anthropic—as an alternative inference mechanism
The
cleaner solution is to make vendor selection explicit:
add a `Vendor` field to `LevelConfig`, update the resolution logic to check the level config first before

falling back to project-level or Helm defaults, and support per-level vendor overrides in the Helm configuration.

ation—the `*out = *in` assignment copies all struct fields including the new `Vendor` string
, so as long as that line is present in the generated code, the deepcopy

spec, and helm defaults. While I could theoretically detect "

should follow.

creating a complete and explicit configuration.
For

'm considering whether to add a top-level `Vendor` field to `SubagentConfig` as

model.
Looking
at the existing struct definitions, neither `LevelConfig` nor `SubagentConfig` currently has a `Vendor` field, so I'd
need to add it. But since the typical use case is a mixed setup with Claude as
the planner and Codex as the executor, and the per-level `Vendor` override in `LevelConfig` already handles that
pattern, adding it to the top level might be unnecessary complexity.
Actually
, I should add `Vendor` to both structs—`LevelConfig` for
per-level overrides and `SubagentConfig` for operators who want a project-wide vendor setting that isn't Anthropic. Now
let me check the chart templates to finalize the task breakdown.
